output_data.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md references the contract doc at /app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md, which explicitly describes: the API shape (GET /health, POST /v1/plan with exact request/response schema including cycle_guard), the route-rule priority/action logic, lock-group relay semantics, cycle guard/visited-node requirement, SQL parameterization requirement, the three files to fix, and the build/restart commands. All behaviors tested — parameterized SQL, cycle guard, lock/clear rule priority, conjunctive lock matching, lock group relay — are described in the contract.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests cover all specified behaviors: health endpoint, reachable/unreachable paths, cycle_guard always true, SQL parameterization check (test_sql_lookups_are_parameterized), conjunctive lock matching (test_lock_positions_require_conjunction), clear-rule-beats-lock-rule priority (test_clearance_rule_opens_scenic_spur, test_clearance_beats_later_platform_lock), NULL lock_sw1 wildcard (test_platform_lock_blocks_staging_when_sw2_north), lock group relay in both directions (test_lock_group_relay_blocks_arrival_leg, test_lock_group_relay_clears_when_spur_opens, test_spur_hold_relay_blocks_staging_departure, test_scenic_spur_relay_open_after_clearance), and performance bound for cycle guard (test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly).
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a descriptive docstring explaining what it checks (e.g., 'A matching clear rule must unlock the spur before the direct junction leg is gated'). Tests are clearly named and each tests a single, well-scoped behavior. The helpers.py is clean and purpose-built. The overall structure is readable and maintainable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The solution/ and tests/ directories are excluded from the Docker image via .dockerignore. The environment deliberately ships a broken implementation (GraphPathRepository uses string concatenation instead of parameterized queries, PathPlanner lacks visited-node tracking, SwitchRuleHandler has inverted rule logic and premature lock-group application) that an agent must understand and fix by reading the contract and source. Answers cannot be trivially obtained from the environment — the correct paths are emergent from the data and logic, not hardcoded anywhere accessible to the agent.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The contract doc explicitly documents the API schema: request body fields {from, to, switches:{sw1,sw2}} and response fields {reachable:bool, path:[...], cycle_guard:true}. The schema is marked normative with exact field names and types. The DB schema is in schema.sql which is also referenced.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: Python dependencies in requirements.txt are fully pinned with exact versions and SHA256 hashes (generated via uv pip compile --generate-hashes). The base Docker image is pinned to a specific sha256 digest. Maven dependencies for the Java Spring Boot application are managed via pom.xml (not examined in detail but standard Maven practice). apt packages are not version-pinned, which is acceptable per the criteria.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. File references in tests (REPO_JAVA path), API endpoint paths, SQL table/column names, and Java class/method names all appear correct and consistent across all files.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The .dockerignore explicitly excludes both solution/ and tests/ directories from the Docker build context. The Dockerfile COPY commands only copy sql/, docs/, and trailswitch/ into the image — no tests or solution files are included.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solve.sh script copies the three corrected Java source files into place, rebuilds the project with build.sh, starts the service with start.sh, and polls until the service is healthy. It derives the solution by actually compiling and running real code, not by echoing hardcoded answers. The solution files (GraphPathRepository.java, PathPlanner.java, SwitchRuleHandler.java) are genuine implementations that compute results via BFS, JDBC queries, and rule evaluation logic.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not require the agent to produce output files (CSV, JSON, etc.). The agent modifies Java source files and rebuilds a running service — the test verifier checks API responses, not output files. No output file needs to be mentioned in instruction.md.

output_data.formatted_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides strong behavioral coverage of the referee contract
through 12 distinct scenario-based API tests with exact path assertions,
covering all core algorithmic requirements.

Strengths:  Tests verify exact path values (not just reachability booleans) for
diverse switch configurations, ensuring the correct graph traversal logic is
implemented. Tests cover all major contract features: conjunctive lock matching,
ascending priority rule evaluation, lock group relay, cycle guard, and SQL
parameterization.

Weaknesses: No negative/malformed input tests; cycle guard test relies on a
2-second timing threshold rather than a deterministic assertion.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite exercises 12 distinct planning scenarios across the 5-node,
7-edge seeded graph, asserting exact paths for reachable routes and empty
paths for blocked routes. Each core contract requirement — conjunctive lock
positions, ascending-priority first-match rule evaluation, lock group relay
propagation, visited-node cycle guard, and SQL parameterization — has at
least one dedicated correctness test. The small graph size means the test
scenarios collectively force correct implementation of all interacting
subsystems; an agent cannot pass all 12 exact-path assertions without
properly implementing the contract logic.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Cycle guard test uses wall-clock timing
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:89-95
Problem: test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly asserts elapsed < 2.0 seconds
         rather than checking a deterministic property of the algorithm.
         Under heavy container load this could flake, though the 2-second
         budget is generous for a 5-node BFS.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert result["cycle_guard"] is True                                       │
│  assert elapsed < 2.0                                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Timing assertion is acceptable given the generous budget; optionally     │
│  # add a visited-count assertion if the API exposed it. No change needed.   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: With only 5 nodes even a naive re-enqueueing BFS would likely
finish under 2 seconds, making this a weak proxy for proper visited-set
tracking. However, the cyclic edge E→C could cause exponential blowup
without cycle detection on deeper graphs. For this seed data the risk is
minimal — the depth-bounded buggy code already passes in time, but fails
on correctness (wrong paths) caught by other tests.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. No test for unknown/invalid station input
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py (entire file)
Problem: The contract says path planning operates over the station graph,
         but no test verifies behavior when an invalid station ID is
         provided (e.g., "Z"). This is a minor secondary gap since the
         instruction doesn't explicitly define error behavior.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  (no test exists)                                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_unknown_station_returns_unreachable():                             │
│      result = plan("Z", "E", {"sw1": "south", "sw2": "south"})              │
│      assert result["reachable"] is False                                    │
│      assert result["path"] == []                                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: This is a minor edge-case gap. The instruction does not
explicitly require specific error handling for invalid stations, and the
natural behavior of BFS over an empty adjacency list is to return
unreachable. This does not enable any shortcut exploitation.

================================================================================

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires debugging a Spring Boot "TrailSwitch" railway graph API that
runs over PostgreSQL. The agent must fix three Java classes
(GraphPathRepository, PathPlanner, SwitchRuleHandler) to comply with a referee
contract defining route-rule evaluation semantics, lock-group relaying, cycle
guards, and parameterized SQL. The solution corrects SQL injection, sort order,
BFS visited-set, and rule-matching logic. The test suite has 13 behavioral
tests covering path reachability, lock/clear semantics, lock-group relay, cycle
termination, and SQL parameterization.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction is Extremely Terse
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 1)
Problem: The entire instruction is a single sentence. While it references the
         contract doc at /app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md, the task
         instruction itself provides no indication of what is broken or what
         "compliance" means. Agents must discover the bugs entirely by reading
         the contract and diffing against current behavior — this is valid for a
         hard task but the instruction lacks explicit success criteria.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  The TrailSwitch API at `/app/trailswitch` runs over PostgreSQL             │
│  (`/app/sql/schema.sql`, `/app/sql/seed.sql`). Bring                        │
│  `GraphPathRepository`, `PathPlanner`, and `SwitchRuleHandler` into         │
│  compliance with `/app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md`, then          │
│  rebuild with `bash /app/trailswitch/build.sh` and restart with             │
│  `bash /app/trailswitch/start.sh` (`http://127.0.0.1:8080`). Keep the      │
│  existing HTTP JSON shape.                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Consider expanding the instruction to at least mention the
classes have known bugs (SQL concatenation, sort order, missing visited set,
incorrect rule-matching logic) so the agent has an explicit enumeration of
expected compliance areas.

Explanation: While the contract doc is thorough, minimal instructions risk the
agent spending excessive time on discovery. Adding 2-3 sentences summarizing
the defect areas would improve clarity without reducing difficulty.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Category "games" Is Incorrect
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 8)
Problem: The category is set to "games" but this task is a debugging/software-
         engineering task involving fixing a Spring Boot API. The railway theme
         is just a domain model — the actual skill tested is API debugging,
         reading specifications, and fixing Java/SQL code.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  category = "games"                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  category = "debugging"                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The task requires diagnosing and fixing bugs in Java source code
to match a contract specification. "debugging" or "software-engineering" would
accurately reflect the skills being evaluated.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Compiled .class Files Should Be Excluded from Environment
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/trailswitch/target/classes/ (directory)

Current approach: The environment ships pre-compiled .class files under
trailswitch/target/classes/. Since build.sh runs `mvn package` which
recompiles from source, these artifact files are redundant dead weight that
inflates the Docker image.

Suggested improvement: Add a .dockerignore entry or remove the target/
directory from the environment to keep the image lean. The Dockerfile already
runs build.sh which compiles everything from source.

Rationale: Shipping stale compiled artifacts is confusing and wastes ~50KB of
image space. It could also mislead agents into thinking the compiled code is
the authoritative version.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

A well-crafted debugging task with a rich domain model, multiple interacting
bugs across three files, and a thorough behavioral test suite. The environment
setup (PostgreSQL, Maven, Spring Boot) is solid and offline-compatible, with
all dependencies pre-installed at build time.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Multi-bug task requiring deep contract reading and cross-file reasoning
  ✓ Excellent test coverage with clear docstrings explaining each scenario
  ✓ Proper offline setup: all deps in Dockerfile, test.sh writes reward

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Category "games" misrepresents the actual skill being evaluated
  ✗ Instruction is a single sentence with no explicit success criteria

Evaluates: Java debugging, specification compliance, graph algorithm
           correctness, SQL security (parameterized queries)

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Fix the category to "debugging" and consider expanding the instruction
  slightly. The task is otherwise well-designed and ready after these changes.
================================================================================
output_data.rubric:
# Rubric 1
Agent uses parameterized SQL query (?) in GraphPathRepository.loadOutgoing instead of string concatenation, +5
Agent changes route_rules ORDER BY to ascending (ASC) priority in GraphPathRepository.loadRules, +3
Agent fixes loadLockGroups to accumulate all edges per group_id rather than overwriting previous entries, +3
Agent implements conjunctive (AND) rule matching in SwitchRuleHandler — all non-null lock positions must match the lineup for the rule to fire, +5
Agent makes SwitchRuleHandler respect the rule_action field, treating 'clear' as opening the edge and 'lock' as blocking it, +5
Agent moves lock group propagation (applyLockGroups) to execute after individual rule evaluation completes, not before, +3
Agent adds visited-node tracking to PathPlanner BFS to prevent infinite re-enqueue on the cyclic graph (E→C return edge), +5
Agent successfully rebuilds the project with build.sh and restarts with start.sh so the service is running with the fixed code, +3
Agent verifies the running API responds correctly by calling /health or /v1/plan after restart, +2
Agent reads and understands the referee contract document before making code changes, +2
Agent preserves the existing HTTP JSON response shape (reachable, path, cycle_guard fields), +1
Agent introduces SQL injection or removes parameterization that was already correct, -5
Agent breaks the build so the service cannot start (compilation errors left unresolved), -3
Agent only marks edge as decided when a rule actually matches (does not prematurely decide on non-matching rules), +2

output_data.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md references the referee contract at /app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md, which explicitly documents the three bugs to fix (SQL parameterization, cycle guard with visited-node tracking, cycle_guard must be true), the exact API shape (GET /health returning {"status":"ok"}, POST /v1/plan with its request/response fields), and file names to edit. The tests check all these behaviors (health endpoint, reachable/path/cycle_guard fields, SQL parameterization check). The contract document is clearly identified as the normative spec and the instruction points to it.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The test suite covers all key behaviors described in the contract: health endpoint (test_health_endpoint), path planning with various switch configurations (test_plan_reaches_arrival_terminal, test_clearance_rule_opens_scenic_spur, test_route_lock_blocks_staging_spur, etc.), cycle_guard being true (test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly), SQL parameterization (test_sql_lookups_are_parameterized), lock group relay semantics (test_lock_group_relay_blocks_arrival_leg, test_lock_group_relay_clears_when_spur_opens), conjunctive lock matching (test_lock_positions_require_conjunction), and NULL lock_sw1 handling (test_platform_lock_blocks_staging_when_sw2_north). The contract says keep the HTTP JSON shape, and the tests verify the exact schema.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a clear, descriptive docstring that explains what it checks (e.g., "Under south/south switches the depot can reach arrival via the junction", "A NULL lock_sw1 rule must still lock when the listed sw2 position matches"). The test file is logically organized: it starts with health, then exercises each route/lock scenario in turn, then checks structural properties (cycle guard timing, SQL parameterization). The helpers.py cleanly separates the service startup logic and the plan() call wrapper, making the test logic easy to read.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The .dockerignore explicitly excludes solution/ and tests/ directories from the Docker image, so the agent cannot access them at runtime. The environment contains intentionally broken Java source files (SQL string concatenation in GraphPathRepository, missing visited-set in PathPlanner, broken OR-logic and premature applyLockGroups in SwitchRuleHandler) that must be genuinely fixed by editing the Java source and rebuilding. There are no shortcut data files or pre-computed answer tables. The policy_expectations table in the schema is empty and unused, so it does not leak answers. Network access is disabled (allow_internet = false).
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The referee contract (trailswitch-referee-contract.md) documents the exact API schema: GET /health returns {"status":"ok"}, POST /v1/plan takes {"from", "to", "switches":{"sw1","sw2"}} and returns {"reachable":bool, "path":[...], "cycle_guard":true}. Field names, types, and required values are marked as normative. The SQL schema.sql also explicitly defines all table structures with column names and types.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: Python dependencies in requirements.txt are pinned with exact versions and SHA256 hashes (using --require-hashes). The base Docker image is pinned to a specific digest (sha256:01f42367...). The requirements.in pins top-level packages to exact versions (pytest==8.2.2, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5, requests==2.32.3). Maven dependencies are managed via pom.xml (not examined for pin state, but this is a Java/Maven project where versions in pom.xml are standard practice and apt packages generally need not be pinned).
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in file paths, variable names, commands, or field names. The referee contract field names ({"from","to","switches"}) match exactly what the RefereeController.PlanRequest record expects (from(), to(), switches()). The test helpers use the same field names. Build and start scripts reference correct paths. The REPO_JAVA path in helpers.py matches the actual file location.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The .dockerignore explicitly lists solution/ and tests/ in its exclusion list. The Dockerfile COPY commands only copy sql/, docs/, and trailswitch/ directories — neither tests/ nor solution/ are copied into the image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution (solve.sh) copies three fixed Java source files into the project and then runs build.sh and start.sh to compile and start the service. The solution files (GraphPathRepository.java, PathPlanner.java, SwitchRuleHandler.java) contain real, fully-implemented corrected Java code that fixes the actual bugs in the environment (parameterized queries, visited-set for cycle detection, correct rule conjunction/priority logic). This demonstrates the actual steps an agent would perform: edit source files, rebuild, restart.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: This task does not require the agent to produce output files. The agent must edit existing Java source files and rebuild/restart the service. The instruction and contract mention the specific Java files to edit (GraphPathRepository, PathPlanner, SwitchRuleHandler) and the build/start commands. There are no CSV/JSON output files required.

output_data.formatted_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides strong correctness coverage of the core path-planning
and rule-engine logic through 12 scenario-based tests with exact path
assertions, and the agent cannot access test expectations to shortcut the work.

Strengths:  Tests verify exact paths (not just reachability) across diverse
switch configurations, covering all major rule interactions including clear
actions, lock groups, conjunction semantics, and cycle termination.

Weaknesses: The contract document is minimal about rule semantics, leaving
the detailed behavior (priority ordering, clear-beats-lock, conjunction,
lock group relay timing) to be inferred from schema and seed data rather
than being explicitly specified.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 tests covering health checks, parameterized SQL,
cycle guard performance, and 9 distinct path-planning scenarios. Each
scenario asserts exact reachability and path values, making it impossible
to pass without a correct implementation of switch-edge activation, rule
priority with clear/lock semantics, lock position conjunction, and lock
group relay. The agent cannot read test files and would need to implement
the full rule engine to produce correct results for arbitrary inputs. The
only minor concern is that the contract document itself is sparse on rule
semantics, relying on the agent to infer correct behavior from the data
model.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Contract underspecifies rule engine semantics
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   environment/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md:9-11
Problem: The contract states that route_rules and lock_groups "define the
railway graph and lock semantics" but does not explicitly describe: (a) that
rules are evaluated in priority order, (b) that 'clear' overrides subsequent
locks, (c) that lock positions use AND conjunction, (d) that lock groups
relay AFTER individual rule evaluation. Tests encode all these behaviors.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  result = plan("A", "E", {"sw1": "south", "sw2": "south"})                 │
│  assert result["path"] == ["A", "C", "E"]  # encodes conjunction semantics  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Add explicit rule-engine specification to the contract document:            │
│  - Rules evaluated ascending by priority (lower = first)                    │
│  - 'clear' action opens an edge; 'lock' blocks it                           │
│  - Both lock_sw1 AND lock_sw2 must match (NULL = don't-care)                │
│  - Lock groups relay after individual rule evaluation                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: While this doesn't make tests exploitable (the agent still
can't read test expectations), it means a diligent agent might implement
a plausible but incorrect interpretation of the rules. This is a task
design concern rather than a test-suite vulnerability — the ambiguity
makes the task harder but doesn't create a shortcut path.

================================================================================

output_data.text_summary:
Difficulty: ✅ HARD

Status: ✅ Solvable (all tests passed by at least one agent run)

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-8: 20.0% (1/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-5: 80.0% (4/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-8: 4 other
  • terminus-gpt5-5: 1 other

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_health_endpoint: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_plan_reaches_arrival_terminal: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_route_lock_blocks_staging_spur: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_platform_lock_blocks_staging_when_sw2_north: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_lock_group_relay_blocks_arrival_leg: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_spur_hold_relay_blocks_staging_departure: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_sql_lookups_are_parameterized: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_lock_positions_require_conjunction: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_clearance_rule_opens_scenic_spur: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_clearance_beats_later_platform_lock: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_lock_group_relay_clears_when_spur_opens: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_scenic_spur_relay_open_after_clearance: 5 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ❌ FAIL, ## Job Summary: TrailSwitch Spring Boot Bug-Fix Task

### 1. Overall Results

**5 trials, 0 passed (reward = 0.0 on all).** All agents achieved 9/13 tests passing — none reached the 13/13 required for a non-zero reward under the all-or-nothing grading scheme. No trials used different models; all appear to be the same agent type on the same task.

---

### 2. Common Failure Pattern

**All 5 trials failed on the exact same 4 tests**, all involving `clear` rule semantics with `sw1=south, sw2=north`:
- `test_clearance_rule_opens_scenic_spur`
- `test_clearance_beats_later_platform_lock`
- `test_lock_group_relay_clears_when_spur_opens`
- `test_scenic_spur_relay_open_after_clearance`

The root issue: `r_release_d` (priority 7, `clear`) should win over `r_platform_d` (priority 8, `lock`) for edge `e_b_d`. Each agent arrived at the correct 9/13 fixes independently but implemented the `clear`/`lock` priority interaction differently — and all were wrong:

| Trial | Specific `clear`-rule failure mode |
|---|---|
| `WyCvDp9` | AND-matching makes `r_platform_d` (NULL `lock_sw1`) match, higher priority wins — wrong |
| `42eXWpp` | AND-logic + cascaded `applyLockGroups` re-locks cleared edges |
| `TywEsz4` | `decidedEdges` tracking: once the priority-8 `lock` fires, `clear` can't override |
| `MLgZXXR` | `clear` matches but doesn't mark `edgeDecided`, so a lower-priority `lock` fires anyway |
| `g8F5qwM` | `ORDER BY rule_priority DESC` — priority 8 evaluated before priority 7 |

---

### 3. Hack Check ✅ Clean

**No cheating detected in any trial.** All 5 reward_hacking checks passed. Agents only edited the three permitted Java source files, ran the official `build.sh`/`start.sh`, and tested via HTTP. No access to `solution/`, no test file modifications, no writes to `reward.txt`/`reward.json`.

---

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues

**Critical underspecification — confirmed across all 5 trials.** Four of five `task_specification` checks explicitly **failed**; one was a borderline pass. The contract (`trailswitch-referee-contract.md`) never documents:

1. **Priority ordering direction**: Is `rule_priority=1` highest or lowest precedence? (`g8F5qwM` got this backwards)
2. **`clear` vs `lock` interaction**: Does a `clear` match "consume" the edge slot so no subsequent `lock` fires? Does `clear` take absolute precedence over `lock` regardless of priority? (`WyCvDp9`, `TywEsz4`, `MLgZXXR`)
3. **NULL column matching semantics**: Does `NULL lock_sw1` mean "match any sw1"? (`WyCvDp9`)

The only way to derive correct semantics is to reverse-engineer from seed data column names and test names (e.g., `test_clearance_beats_later_platform_lock`), not the contract itself. This is the single systemic root cause for all failures.

---

### 5. Progress on Failed Trials

All agents were **very close** — 69% test pass rate (9/13) universally:

- ✅ **SQL injection fix** (parameterized queries in `loadOutgoing`): All 5 correct
- ✅ **Lock group aggregation fix** (`computeIfAbsent` instead of `put` overwrite): All 5 correct  
- ✅ **Visited-set cycle guard in `PathPlanner`**: All 5 correct
- ❌ **`clear`/`lock` rule priority semantics in `SwitchRuleHandler`**: All 5 wrong, each in a different way

The three structural/algorithmic bugs were solved universally. Only the behaviorally ambiguous `SwitchRuleHandler` logic blocked all trials.

---

### 6. Key Differences Between Trials

No model-level differences are apparent (likely same model on all). The variation is purely in *how* each agent got the `clear` semantics wrong — suggesting the agents explored genuinely different solution strategies and all hit the same specification wall from different angles. This actually strengthens the conclusion that the spec is the blocker, not agent capability.

---

### Recommendation

**Fix the contract document** before re-running. Add explicit documentation for:
- Priority ordering (e.g., "lower `rule_priority` value = evaluated first; first matching rule wins")
- Clear-action semantics (e.g., "`rule_action='clear'` marks the edge as unlocked and consumes the decision slot, preventing any subsequent rule from re-locking it")
- NULL column matching (e.g., "a NULL `lock_sw1` constraint matches any switch position")

With these additions, all three correct structural fixes suggest agents would likely reach 13/13.

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires fixing three Java classes in a Spring Boot railway-graph
API (TrailSwitch) to comply with a referee contract. The agent must fix SQL
injection (parameterize queries), correct rule-matching logic (conjunction
instead of disjunction), add cycle-guard visited-set tracking, fix lock-group
relay ordering, and correct rule priority sort order. The test suite contains
13 well-documented behavioral tests that call the running API and verify path
planning results under various switch configurations.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction.md is Extremely Brief
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 1)
Problem: The entire task instruction is a single sentence. While it references
         the contract document at /app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md,
         the instruction itself provides minimal context about what is actually
         broken or what "compliance" entails. Agents must infer the full scope
         of work from the contract doc alone.

Current approach: A single line mentioning the three files, the contract, and
the build/restart commands.

Suggested fix: Expand the instruction to at least briefly enumerate the known
defects or compliance areas (SQL parameterization, rule matching semantics,
cycle detection, lock group relay logic) so the agent has a clearer starting
point. The contract doc can remain the authoritative reference.

Explanation: A more descriptive instruction helps agents prioritize and
understand the scope without trial-and-error. One sentence for a "hard" task
with 5+ distinct bugs is unusually terse.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Category "games" is Misleading
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 7)
Problem: The task is categorized as "games" but it is a debugging/code-repair
         task on a Spring Boot API with graph traversal and SQL. There is no
         game logic, player interaction, or game simulation involved.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  category = "games"                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  category = "debugging"                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The task involves diagnosing and repairing bugs in existing Java
source code. "debugging" accurately describes the work. "games" should be
reserved for tasks involving game logic, puzzles, or simulations.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Add Explicit Contract Rules to Instruction
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md

Current approach: The contract specifies "must bind station ids as SQL
parameters" and "must track visited nodes", but does not explicitly document
the rule-matching semantics (conjunction of lock_sw1 AND lock_sw2), the
"clear" action semantics, or the lock-group relay ordering that the tests
verify. These are the hardest parts of the task.

Suggested improvement: Add a "Rule evaluation semantics" section to the
contract that specifies: (1) rules are evaluated in ascending priority order,
(2) a two-position rule fires only when BOTH switches match (conjunction),
(3) "clear" action marks an edge decided-but-not-locked, and (4) lock groups
relay AFTER individual rule evaluation completes.

Rationale: Without these details documented, the task relies on the agent
reverse-engineering the intended semantics purely from buggy code — which
may be intentional for a "hard" task, but risks the task being unsolvable
without lucky guessing when the contract is the stated authoritative source.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

A well-constructed debugging task with a thoughtful multi-bug design that
tests graph algorithms, SQL safety, and rule-engine logic in a realistic
Spring Boot codebase. The test suite is comprehensive with clear docstrings.
The main weakness is that the instruction and contract don't fully specify
the semantics the tests enforce.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Multiple orthogonal bugs requiring distinct reasoning (SQL injection,
    algorithm correctness, rule semantics, data structure usage)
  ✓ Excellent test suite with 13 descriptive behavioral tests
  ✓ Clean Dockerfile with all dependencies properly installed at build time

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Instruction is a single line for a hard multi-bug task
  ✗ Category metadata is inaccurate ("games" vs "debugging")

Evaluates: Java debugging, graph algorithm repair, SQL injection remediation,
           rule-engine logic comprehension

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Change category to "debugging" and expand instruction.md to provide more
  context. Optionally enrich the contract with the rule-evaluation semantics
  that the tests actually enforce.
================================================================================

# Rubric 1
Agent reads the referee contract document to understand the required behavior before making changes, +2
Agent reads or inspects the existing source files (GraphPathRepository, PathPlanner, SwitchRuleHandler) to identify bugs before editing, +2
Agent fixes GraphPathRepository.loadOutgoing to use parameterized SQL with placeholder instead of string concatenation, +5
Agent fixes GraphPathRepository.loadRules to sort by rule_priority ASC (not DESC), +3
Agent fixes GraphPathRepository.loadLockGroups to accumulate multiple edges per group using computeIfAbsent or equivalent (not overwriting with put), +3
Agent fixes PathPlanner to track visited stations in a Set to prevent infinite loops on the cyclic graph (E to C return edge), +5
Agent ensures PathPlanner always returns cycle_guard as true when planning finishes normally, +2
Agent fixes SwitchRuleHandler.ruleMatches to use AND logic (both sw1 and sw2 conditions must match) instead of OR logic, +5
Agent fixes SwitchRuleHandler to call applyLockGroups AFTER computing locked edges from rules (not before on empty set), +3
Agent fixes SwitchRuleHandler to distinguish between lock and clear rule actions when processing matched rules, +3
Agent successfully rebuilds the project with build.sh without compilation errors, +3
Agent restarts the service with start.sh and verifies it responds on port 8080 (e.g., checking health endpoint), +2
Agent tests the /v1/plan endpoint with sample requests to verify correct reachability behavior, +1
Agent modifies the HTTP JSON response shape or API endpoints, breaking the contract requirement to keep them intact, -5
Agent introduces SQL injection vulnerability by keeping or adding string concatenation for station IDs in queries, -3
Agent removes or incorrectly implements cycle detection, allowing potential infinite loops, -3

output_data.formatted_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite comprehensively verifies the core algorithmic requirements through
exact path and reachability assertions across diverse switch configurations, and
no shortcut solution exists that bypasses the real implementation.

Strengths:  12 behavioral tests with exact expected paths cover all major bugs
in the provided code (rule ordering, AND conjunction, clear action, lock groups,
SQL parameterization). The live API approach prevents hardcoding.

Weaknesses: The cycle_guard / visited-node requirement is only weakly verified
since the small graph terminates quickly even with a depth-limit approach.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite exercises the TrailSwitch API through POST /v1/plan calls with
varied switch positions, checking exact reachability and path values. It covers
rule priority ordering, AND conjunction for lock matching, clear-action
short-circuiting, lock-group propagation, and SQL parameterization via source
inspection. The tests are tightly coupled to the seed data's specific topology,
producing deterministic expected values that cannot be satisfied without
correctly implementing the contract's algorithm. The only gap is that the
visited-node tracking requirement is not rigorously distinguished from a
depth-limit approach on this small 5-node graph.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Cycle guard verified only by timing, not by mechanism
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:89-95
Problem: The contract requires "track visited nodes so search terminates with
cycle_guard true." The test checks elapsed < 2.0 and cycle_guard is True, but
the existing buggy code (depth-limit of 12, no visited set) already satisfies
both conditions on this 5-node graph. An agent could leave the depth-limit
approach in place without implementing true visited-node tracking.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  assert result["cycle_guard"] is True                                      │
│  assert elapsed < 2.0                                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add a source-level check analogous to the SQL parameterization test:    │
│  source = PLANNER_JAVA.read_text(encoding="utf-8")                         │
│  assert "visited" in source.lower() or "seen" in source.lower()            │
│  # Or test with a larger graph where depth-limit fails                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Since the graph has only 5 nodes and MAX_DEPTH=12, a BFS with a
depth cap terminates in microseconds and produces correct shortest paths. An
agent that fixes all other bugs but leaves the depth-limit cycle prevention
would pass all tests. This is a secondary concern because the functional
behavior is identical for the tested graph — both approaches produce correct
paths and terminate promptly.

================================================================================

output_data.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md directs the agent to fix three named Java files to comply with the referee contract at /app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md. That contract document explicitly covers all behaviors tested: ascending rule priority, AND-conjunction for NULL lock fields, 'clear' action stopping further rule evaluation, lock group relay semantics, visited-node cycle guard, and parameterized SQL. The API shape (GET /health, POST /v1/plan with exact request/response fields) is also specified. All test scenarios are grounded in the documented contract.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Tests cover all behaviors described in the contract: health endpoint, multiple plan routing scenarios testing rule priority, clear action, AND conjunction, lock group relay, cycle guard with timing, SQL parameterization check (source-code inspection), and the 'reachable:false + empty path' case. No described behavior is left untested.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a descriptive name (e.g., test_clearance_beats_later_platform_lock, test_lock_group_relay_blocks_arrival_leg) and a docstring explaining precisely what contract rule is being validated. The structure is readable and maintainable, making it clear what each assertion targets.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The Dockerfile copies only sql/, docs/, and trailswitch/ into the image — solution/ and tests/ are excluded. The agent cannot see the corrected source files or test assertions at runtime. Tests verify live API behavior and even directly inspect the source file to check SQL parameterization, so the agent must genuinely fix the code rather than relying on static data. The seed data in seed.sql cannot be used to trivially shortcut the behavioral tests.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The referee contract doc explicitly specifies the API schema: GET /health returns {"status":"ok"}, POST /v1/plan accepts {"from", "to", "switches":{"sw1","sw2"}} and returns {"reachable":bool, "path":[...], "cycle_guard":true}. All fields and their types are normatively defined.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: requirements.txt is generated by uv with exact version pins and SHA-256 hashes for every package (certifi, charset-normalizer, pytest, requests, etc.). This satisfies the pinning requirement for Python/pip packages.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across instruction.md, task.toml, Dockerfile, SQL files, test files, solution files, or Java source files.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies sql/, docs/, and trailswitch/ into /app. Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the image. The agent will not have access to these at runtime.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh copies corrected Java source files and rebuilds/restarts the service via the existing build/start scripts. The solution files contain actual algorithmic implementations: BFS with a visited-set for cycle prevention, correct rule evaluation loop with ascending priority and clear/lock logic, AND-conjunction matching, proper lock group aggregation, and parameterized SQL queries. No answer is hardcoded or echoed directly.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not require the agent to produce any output files for tests to check. Tests verify live HTTP API behavior and inspect an existing source file. No output file filename needs to be specified in instruction.md.

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                           REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires agents to debug and fix a Spring Boot railway graph
API ("TrailSwitch") backed by PostgreSQL. Three Java files contain bugs:
GraphPathRepository uses SQL string concatenation instead of parameters,
SwitchRuleHandler applies lock groups before per-edge rules and uses OR
logic instead of AND for rule matching, and PathPlanner lacks proper
cycle-guard (visited-set) tracking. The agent must bring these classes
into compliance with a provided referee contract, rebuild, and restart.
The test suite validates 12 behavioral scenarios covering route planning,
lock groups, clearance rules, cycle detection, and SQL parameterization.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Timing-Based Test Assertion
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py (lines 89-95)
Problem: test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly asserts elapsed < 2.0 seconds.
         While this is a generous threshold and primarily guards against
         infinite loops rather than measuring performance, it is still
         sensitive to hardware speed and container resource limits.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly():                                   │
│      """Planning on the cyclic seed graph must finish without exhaustive    │
│      re-enqueue loops."""                                                   │
│      start = time.time()                                                    │
│      result = plan("C", "E", {"sw1": "south", "sw2": "south"})             │
│      elapsed = time.time() - start                                          │
│      assert result["cycle_guard"] is True                                   │
│      assert elapsed < 2.0                                                   │
│  ...                                                                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Increase the threshold significantly (e.g., 10s or 30s) to
make it purely a hang-detection guard, or remove the timing assertion and
rely solely on the cycle_guard boolean plus the verifier timeout to catch
infinite loops.

Explanation: A correct BFS on 5 nodes finishes in milliseconds, so 2s is
very generous. However, under heavy container load or minimal CPU
allocation, JVM warm-up and GC pauses could theoretically push past 2s.
A 10-30s threshold would still catch runaway loops without risking false
failures on slow hardware.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Category Mismatch
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 7)
Problem: The task is categorized as "games" but is actually a debugging
         task on a Spring Boot Java API with PostgreSQL. The railway
         theming is cosmetic — the core work is fixing JDBC queries,
         correcting rule-evaluation logic, and adding cycle detection.

Current approach: `category = "games"`

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  category = "debugging"                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The "games" category is intended for game logic, puzzles, and
simulations. This task is fundamentally a code repair/debugging exercise
requiring understanding of JDBC, graph algorithms, and business rule
logic. "debugging" better reflects the skills being tested.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Brevity
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 1)

Current approach: The entire instruction is a single sentence directing
the agent to a contract document. While the contract is thorough, the
instruction itself provides minimal framing for the debugging context.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # TrailSwitch API Bug Fix                                                  │
│                                                                             │
│  The TrailSwitch railway route-planning API at `/app/trailswitch` runs      │
│  over PostgreSQL (`/app/sql/schema.sql`, `/app/sql/seed.sql`). Three        │
│  service classes contain bugs that violate the referee contract.             │
│                                                                             │
│  Bring `GraphPathRepository`, `PathPlanner`, and `SwitchRuleHandler`        │
│  into compliance with `/app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md`, then     │
│  rebuild with `bash /app/trailswitch/build.sh` and restart with             │
│  `bash /app/trailswitch/start.sh` (`http://127.0.0.1:8080`).               │
│  Keep the existing HTTP JSON shape.                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: Adding a brief heading and line breaks improves readability
without changing the content. The current single-line format is functional
but could be more scannable for agents.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

A well-constructed debugging task that requires deep reasoning about graph
traversal, rule-evaluation semantics, and SQL injection prevention. The
seeded bugs are subtle and interrelated, making this a genuinely hard task
that tests real software engineering judgment.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Multiple interacting bugs requiring holistic understanding of the system
  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with clear docstrings and full behavior coverage
  ✓ Proper offline setup — all deps in Dockerfile, no network at test time

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Timing assertion in tests could be fragile on constrained hardware
  ✗ Category "games" misrepresents the actual debugging nature of the task

Evaluates: Java debugging, JDBC/SQL security, graph algorithm correctness,
           business rule logic comprehension

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  The task is high-quality and nearly ready. Fix the category to
  "debugging" and consider relaxing the 2-second timing threshold to
  avoid false failures on slow runners.
================================================================================
output_data.rubric:
# Rubric 1
Agent reads and understands the referee contract document before making code changes, +2
Agent fixes SQL injection vulnerability in GraphPathRepository.loadOutgoing by using parameterized query (? placeholder) instead of string concatenation, +5
Agent changes route_rules ORDER BY from DESC to ASC in GraphPathRepository.loadRules to evaluate rules in ascending priority, +3
Agent fixes loadLockGroups to properly accumulate all edges per group_id (using computeIfAbsent or equivalent) instead of overwriting with a single-element set, +3
Agent introduces unnecessary changes to files not mentioned in the task (e.g., modifying RefereeController, model classes, or schema), -2

# Rubric 2
Agent fixes ruleMatches to use AND conjunction semantics: both non-NULL lock_sw1 and lock_sw2 must match the request switches simultaneously, +5
Agent implements proper per-edge rule evaluation: stop processing further rules for an edge after the first matching rule, +3
Agent respects rule_action field: 'lock' blocks the edge, 'clear' leaves it open and stops further rules on that edge, +3
Agent moves applyLockGroups call to execute AFTER per-edge rule evaluation rather than before (fixing the ordering bug), +3
Agent removes the bug where edgeDecided was set unconditionally regardless of match, causing non-matching rules to block subsequent rule evaluation for an edge, +2

# Rubric 3
Agent adds a visited/seen set to the BFS in PathPlanner to prevent infinite traversal on the cyclic graph, +5
Agent ensures nodes are only enqueued once by checking the visited set before adding to the queue, +3
Agent preserves the existing API JSON response shape (reachable, path, cycle_guard fields), +2
Agent successfully rebuilds the project with build.sh after making code changes, +2
Agent restarts the service with start.sh and verifies it responds on port 8080 (e.g., curling /health), +3
Agent attempts to start the service without first killing an existing instance occupying port 8080, causing startup failure without recovery, -2

output_data.formatted_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite comprehensively verifies all core algorithmic constraints through
behavioral assertions against a live API, and no shortcut solution exists that
avoids implementing the correct algorithm.

Strengths:  Tests verify exact paths and reachability for carefully chosen switch
combinations that exercise each rule-evaluation semantic (conjunction, clearance,
priority ordering, lock group relay). Source-code inspection enforces SQL
parameterization.

Weaknesses: The depth-cap-at-12 policy expectation is only tested implicitly via
a timing assertion rather than a scenario that exercises path rejection at depth
boundary.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 focused tests that cover all 7 policy expectations
from the seed data plus the API shape. Each test calls POST /v1/plan with a
specific (from, to, switches) triple and asserts the exact reachable flag and
path list. The scenarios are selected so that each bug in the broken starter
code (OR-logic in ruleMatches, wrong rule sort order, lock groups applied
before rules, loadLockGroups dropping members, missing visited-set) causes at
least one test to fail. The agent cannot shortcut because tests run against a
live HTTP endpoint and the agent cannot see which inputs are tested.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Depth cap boundary not directly tested
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py (no specific test)
Problem: The policy_expectations row says "cap depth at 12" but no test
         creates a scenario where a valid path of length >12 would exist if
         the cap were absent. The timing test (test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly)
         only verifies the search terminates within 2 seconds and cycle_guard
         is true — it does not verify that an overly-deep path is rejected.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly():                                   │
│      start = time.time()                                                    │
│      result = plan("C", "E", {"sw1": "south", "sw2": "south"})             │
│      elapsed = time.time() - start                                          │
│      assert result["cycle_guard"] is True                                   │
│      assert elapsed < 2.0                                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add a graph scenario (or note that the 5-node seed graph makes paths    │
│  # >12 impossible, so depth=12 is effectively untestable without schema    │
│  # changes. The timing test is a reasonable proxy for this seed.)           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: With only 5 nodes and cycle_guard preventing revisits, no path
can exceed length 5 regardless of depth cap. The depth=12 cap is therefore
untestable on this graph — a solution that sets MAX_DEPTH=5 or MAX_DEPTH=999
would pass identically. This is minor because the cycle-guard visited-set
requirement IS tested (test_cycle_guard_finishes_quickly would timeout without
it on the cyclic E→C edge), and the depth cap is a secondary safeguard.

================================================================================

output_data.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md references the referee contract at `/app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md` (normative) and the `policy_expectations` rows in the seed data (also normative). The contract explicitly documents the API shape (GET /health, POST /v1/plan with full request/response shapes), the cycle_guard behavior, SQL parameterization requirement, and route-lock semantics. The seed data's `policy_expectations` table enumerates conjunction logic, clearance rules, lock group relay, and loadLockGroups correctness — all of which are tested. The behavioral details tested (specific path assertions, lock rule interactions, SQL parameterization check) derive from these normative documents rather than being undocumented.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: Tests cover: health endpoint, multiple path-planning scenarios exercising switch combinations (south/south, south/north, north/north), clearance rules, lock group relay in both directions, NULL-column lock rules, cycle_guard flag, SQL parameterization (source code check), and conjunction matching. The policy_expectations rows (sql_parameterized, cycle_guard, rule_priority, lock_conjunction, clearance_rule, lock_group_relay, lock_group_members) all have corresponding test cases. The described behavior in the contract is well-exercised.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a clearly descriptive docstring (e.g., 'A matching clear rule must unlock the spur before the direct junction leg is gated.', 'A NULL lock_sw1 rule must still lock when the listed sw2 position matches.'). Function names are descriptive and map to the policy being tested. The test file uses a module-scoped fixture for service startup. The structure is clean and maintainable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The `.dockerignore` explicitly excludes `solution/` and `tests/` from the Docker image. The environment contains intentionally buggy code in all three target files (GraphPathRepository uses string concatenation and wrong ORDER BY; SwitchRuleHandler applies lock groups before rules, misses 'clear' action, and uses disjunction instead of conjunction; PathPlanner lacks visited-node tracking). The `test_sql_lookups_are_parameterized` test reads the agent's actual modified source file, so the agent must produce genuinely parameterized code. No answers are embedded in accessible environment files.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The referee contract at `/app/docs/trailswitch-referee-contract.md` explicitly defines: `GET /health → {"status":"ok"}` and `POST /v1/plan` with exact request shape `{"from", "to", "switches":{"sw1","sw2"}}` and response shape `{"reachable":bool, "path":[...], "cycle_guard":true}`. The DB schema is in schema.sql with full DDL. Both are clearly normative specifications, not just examples.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: Python/pip packages in `requirements.txt` are fully pinned with SHA-256 hashes (generated by `uv pip compile --generate-hashes`). Java dependencies in `pom.xml` pin Spring Boot parent to `3.2.5` and PostgreSQL driver to `42.7.3`. apt packages (openjdk, maven, postgresql) are not pinned, which is acceptable per the criterion.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. File paths in helpers.py (`/app/trailswitch/src/main/java/com/trailswitch/repo/GraphPathRepository.java`) match the actual file in the environment. API paths `/health` and `/v1/plan` are consistent across tests, helpers, and contract. Build/start script paths are correct.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The `.dockerignore` explicitly lists `solution/` and `tests/` as excluded directories. The Dockerfile only COPYs `sql/`, `docs/`, and `trailswitch/` from the environment directory into the image. Neither tests nor solution are present in the runtime image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: The solution files (GraphPathRepository.java, PathPlanner.java, SwitchRuleHandler.java) contain complete algorithmic implementations: BFS with visited-node tracking (HashSet), proper lock-group relay applied after rules, conjunction-based rule matching, clearance rule short-circuiting, and parameterized JDBC queries with `?` placeholders. `solve.sh` copies these files, then runs the real build (`mvn package`) and starts the service. No answers are echoed or cat'd; the service must correctly compute paths at test time.
➖ not_applicable - file_reference_mentioned: The task does not require the agent to produce any output files. The agent modifies Java source files in-place and rebuilds/restarts a service; all verification is done via HTTP API calls. No output file filenames need to be mentioned in instruction.md.

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to debug and fix a Spring Boot railway graph path-
planning API (TrailSwitch) backed by PostgreSQL. The codebase has intentional
bugs in three Java files: SQL injection in GraphPathRepository, incorrect rule
evaluation order/logic in SwitchRuleHandler, and missing visited-node tracking
in PathPlanner. The agent must bring these into compliance with a referee
contract document and policy expectations in the seed data, then rebuild and
restart the service. The test suite validates 12 behavioral scenarios covering
route-lock semantics, cycle guards, lock-group relays, and SQL parameterization.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Category "games" Is Questionable
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/task.toml (line 7)
Problem: The category is set to "games" but this task is a debugging/repair
         task on a Spring Boot API with database interactions. It tests
         software engineering skills (reading contracts, fixing Java code,
         understanding SQL schemas), not game logic or puzzle-solving in a
         traditional sense.

Current approach: `category = "games"`

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  category = "debugging"                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The task requires reading a contract spec, diagnosing bugs in
Java source code (SQL injection, wrong sort order, incorrect boolean logic,
missing visited-set), and applying fixes. "debugging" more accurately
reflects the skills tested. The railway-graph domain is thematic dressing,
not gameplay.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Instruction Is Extremely Terse
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (line 1)
Problem: The entire instruction is a single sentence. While the referee
         contract document and seed data provide the full specification, the
         task prompt itself gives almost no orientation about what is broken
         or what "compliance" means. A junior developer or agent unfamiliar
         with the domain would struggle to know where to start.

Current approach: One sentence referencing three files, a contract document,
and seed data without explicitly stating what bugs exist or what behavioral
properties must hold.

Suggested fix: Expand the instruction to at least mention: (1) the known
defects are in the three named Java files, (2) the policy_expectations table
enumerates the behavioral properties that must hold, and (3) the agent should
read the contract document for the full specification. This preserves the
task's difficulty while providing adequate orientation.

Explanation: Tasks should have clear enough instructions that an agent can
determine success criteria without guessing. The contract document is good
supplementary material, but the root instruction should summarize the goal
and success criteria explicitly.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Add Working-Directory Check to test.sh
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (lines 1-10)

Current approach: test.sh jumps directly into running pytest without the
recommended working-directory safety check.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  #!/usr/bin/env bash                                                        │
│  set -uo pipefail                                                           │
│  mkdir -p /logs/verifier                                                    │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ "$PWD" = "/" ]; then                                                  │
│      echo "Error: No working directory set." >&2                            │
│      exit 1                                                                 │
│  fi                                                                         │
│                                                                             │
│  PYTHONPATH=/tests python3 -m pytest --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json \      │
│      -p no:cacheprovider -rA /tests/test_outputs.py -q                      │
│  rc=$?                                                                      │
│  if [ "$rc" -eq 0 ]; then                                                   │
│    echo 1 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  else                                                                       │
│    echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  fi                                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: The recommended test.sh template includes a WORKDIR check to
provide a clear error message if the Dockerfile's WORKDIR is misconfigured.
This is a minor robustness improvement.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted debugging task with a realistic codebase, intentional
multi-layered bugs, and a thorough behavioral test suite. The environment
setup is solid with proper dependency pinning, offline compliance, and all
required terminal dependencies present.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent test coverage with 12 focused behavioral tests and clear
    docstrings explaining each scenario
  ✓ Well-designed bug constellation requiring understanding of SQL safety,
    sort-order semantics, boolean logic, graph traversal, and lock-group
    relay ordering
  ✓ Proper offline setup: all deps installed at build time, hash-pinned
    requirements, digest-pinned base image, tmux/asciinema present

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Category "games" is a poor fit for what is fundamentally a debugging task
  ✗ Root instruction is too terse — relies heavily on the contract document
    without summarizing success criteria

Evaluates: Java debugging, contract-driven development, graph algorithm
           correctness, SQL injection remediation

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  The task is structurally sound and functionally complete. Fix the category
  to "debugging" and consider expanding the instruction to provide clearer
  orientation before marking as ready.
================================================================================
output_data.rubric:
# Rubric 1
Agent converts GraphPathRepository.loadOutgoing from string concatenation to a parameterized query using ? placeholder and PreparedStatement binding, +5
Agent changes loadRules ORDER BY from rule_priority DESC to rule_priority ASC, +3
Agent fixes loadLockGroups to accumulate all edge_ids per group_id (e.g., using computeIfAbsent or equivalent) instead of overwriting previous entries, +3
Agent reads and understands the policy_expectations seed data before making changes to GraphPathRepository, +1
Agent introduces new SQL injection vulnerabilities or removes the parameterized query after initially fixing it, -5

# Rubric 2
Agent implements conjunctive matching logic where all non-NULL lock_sw columns must equal the corresponding request switch position (NULL means no constraint), +5
Agent implements clear-rule semantics: a matching clear rule leaves the edge unlocked and stops further rule evaluation on that edge, +3
Agent moves applyLockGroups to execute after all per-edge route rules have been evaluated (not before), +3
Agent ensures non-matching rules do not stop evaluation of later rules on the same edge, +2
Agent preserves the lock group relay logic so that when any member edge is locked, all edges in that group become locked, +2
Agent uses disjunctive matching (any single column match triggers the rule) instead of the required conjunctive semantics, -3

# Rubric 3
Agent adds a visited set to the BFS in PathPlanner.plan that prevents re-enqueuing already-visited stations, terminating search on the cyclic graph, +5
Agent retains the MAX_DEPTH cap of 12 to bound search depth as required by the cycle_guard policy, +2
Agent successfully rebuilds the project with build.sh after making code changes, +2
Agent successfully restarts the service with start.sh and confirms it responds at /health endpoint, +3
Agent reads the referee contract document and schema/seed SQL to understand requirements before editing code, +1
Agent removes the depth cap or visited tracking, leaving the path planner vulnerable to infinite loops on cycles, -5
